topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a dull thud. My stomach clenched. I clicked it open, my fingers hovering over the subject line: "Publication Confirmation." My eyes scanned the text, a slow, deliberate movement that felt like wading through thick mud. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My name wasn't there. Instead, a stranger's name was boldly displayed. A sharp, hot spike of annoyance shot through my chest. I reread the attribution, then reread it again, my jaw tightening. Why? This was *my* work. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing in the quiet office. I needed answers, and they needed to be immediate. I started drafting a reply, the words coming out clipped and edged with frustration, every keystroke a deliberate, sharp punctuation mark.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, breathing in short, shallow gasps. The confirmation email was mocking me. It was published. But not by me. My hands began to tremble slightly as I reread the metadata. The discrepancy was glaring, undeniable. A wave of simmering irritation washed over me, making my skin feel prickly. I pushed back from my desk, pacing the small space between the filing cabinet and the window. I wanted to call the editor, but the thought of explaining this absurdity felt exhausting. I tapped my pen rapidly against the desk surface, the rhythmic clicking a frantic counterpoint to the stillness of the room. This couldn't just be a clerical error; this demanded attention, right now.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I felt a sudden, intense tightening in my throat. I leaned back in my chair, my posture stiffening. My gaze darted from the screen to the clock on the wall, a silent tally of the minutes slipping away. The attribution was undeniably incorrect. A slow burn of aggravation started in my gut, spreading outwards until my shoulders tensed up near my ears. I ran a hand through my hair, pulling at it roughly. There was no time for hesitation. I pulled up all the relevant correspondence, my movements becoming jerky and focused. I needed to confront this oversight, and the delay felt intolerable, a heavy weight pressing down on my resolve.
